content: JavaScript Programming Web Development Software Development 5 min read 1 hour ago -- Press enter or click to view image in full size When I first started learning React, one question kept coming to my mind. If React is a Single Page Application, then how do websites have multiple pages like Home, About, Contact, Profile, Dashboard, and Settings? The answer is Routing. Routing is one of the most important concepts in React because almost every real-world application depends on it. Whether it’s Netflix, Amazon, GitHub, or LinkedIn, all of them use routing to navigate between different screens without refreshing the entire page. In this article, I’ll share everything I learned while understanding React Routing from scratch. What is Routing? Routing is simply the process of deciding which component should be displayed for a particular URL. For example, / → Home Page /about → About Page /contact → Contact Page /profile → Profile PageWhen the URL changes, React Router decides which component should be rendered. Why Do We Need Routing? Imagine a React application without routing. Everything would exist inside a single component. App├── Home ├── About ├── Contact ├── Dashboard ├── SettingsAs the application grows, managing everything inside one component becomes almost impossible. Routing helps us divide our application into multiple screens while still keeping the experience smooth. Traditional Websites vs React Applications Traditional websites work differently. User clicks About↓Browser sends request↓Server returns new HTML page↓Browser reloadsEvery navigation reloads the entire page. React applications don’t do this. Instead, User clicks About↓URL Changes↓React Router matches the route↓Only the required component changesThe browser never reloads the entire application. This is why React applications feel much faster. Single Page Application (SPA) React is called a Single Page Application because only one HTML file is loaded initially. After that, JavaScript updates the UI whenever the URL changes. Think of it like changing rooms inside the same house instead of constructing a completely new house every time. Installing React Router The first step is installing React Router. npm install react-router-domThis library gives us everything required for navigation inside React applications. Declarative Routing For a long time, React applications used what is known as Declarative Routing. The idea is very simple. We declare which component belongs to which path. } /> } /> } /> Notice something interesting. We’re not writing any navigation logic. We’re simply declaring the routes. That’s exactly why it’s called Declarative Routing. BrowserRouter BrowserRouter is the component that enables routing inside our React application. Internally, it uses the browser’s History API to update the URL without refreshing the page. Without BrowserRouter, React Router cannot work. Routes and Route Routes works like a manager. Its responsibility is to check the current URL. Every Route represents a possible path. If the current URL matches a route, React renders its corresponding component. Simple. Navigation Instead of using the traditional HTML anchor tag, AboutReact Router provides: AboutWhy? Because an anchor tag refreshes the entire page. Link only updates the URL and lets React handle the rendering. This preserves the SPA experience. NavLink Sometimes we want to know which page is currently active. For example, Home | About | ContactIf the user is on the About page, we usually highlight it. That’s exactly what NavLink does. It automatically knows whether the current route is active. Perfect for navigation bars and sidebars. Dynamic Routes Not every URL is fixed. Imagine Instagram. Every profile has a different URL. /user/devendra/user/john/user/alexInstead of creating thousands of routes, React Router provides Dynamic Routes. } />The value after : becomes a dynamic parameter. useParams() How do we read that dynamic value? Using useParams(). const { username } = useParams();Now visiting /user/devendragives username = "devendra"Very useful for profile pages, product pages, blogs, and dashboards. Nested Routing Large applications usually have layouts. For example, Dashboard├── Profile ├── Orders ├── SettingsInstead of repeating the Dashboard UI on every page, React Router allows us to create Nested Routes. The parent route contains the common layout. The child routes replace only a specific section. Outlet This is where Outlet comes in. Think of Outlet as a placeholder. function Dashboard() { return ( <> ); }Whenever the child route changes, React renders it inside the Outlet. Everything outside the Outlet remains unchanged. useNavigate() Sometimes navigation happens after an action. For example, • Login Successful • Registration Completed • Payment Success Instead of clicking a link, we navigate programmatically. const navigate = useNavigate();navigate("/dashboard"); useLocation() Sometimes we need information about the current URL. const location = useLocation();It provides useful information such as: • Current pathname • Search query • Hash • Navigation state This is commonly used for analytics, breadcrumbs, authentication checks, and conditional layouts. Protected Routes Not every page should be accessible to everyone. Imagine a dashboard. If the user isn’t logged in, they should be redirected to the login page. This concept is called a Protected Route. The idea is simple. User Logged In?↓Yes → DashboardNo → LoginAlthough React Router doesn’t provide protected routes out of the box, we can easily build them using conditional rendering and navigation. The Biggest Limitation Declarative Routing works beautifully for navigation. But modern applications need much more than navigation. Almost every page needs: • API Calls • Form Submission • Error Handling • Data Fetching Traditionally, we handled all of this using useEffect. The flow looked like this: Navigate↓Render Component↓useEffect()↓Fetch Data↓Re-renderIt works. But it isn’t the most efficient approach. This limitation eventually led to one of React Router’s biggest improvements… Data Routing. We’ll explore that in the next article. Final Thoughts Learning React Router completely changed the way I think about React applications. Routing isn’t just about changing pages. It’s about organizing applications, improving user experience, and making navigation feel seamless. Once I understood concepts like nested routes, dynamic routes, Outlet, and programmatic navigation, building larger React applications became much more manageable. In the next part of this series, we’ll dive into Data Routing, understand why React Router introduced it, and see how it solves problems that Declarative Routing couldn’t. Happy Coding❤️.
